note_data.title: Acids, Bases and Salts
keyPoints: Acids are substances that produce hydrogen ions (H+) when dissolved in water
Bases are substances that produce hydroxide ions (OH-) when dissolved in water
Salt is a compound formed by the neutralization reaction between an acid and a base
pH scale ranges from 0 to 14, where 7 is neutral, below 7 is acidic, above 7 is basic
Indicators are substances that change color in acidic and basic solutions
Strong acids completely ionize in water while weak acids partially ionize
Neutralization reaction: Acid + Base → Salt + Water
Common acids include HCl, H2SO4, HNO3 and common bases include NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)2
Salts can be normal, acidic or basic depending on the strength of parent acid and base

definitions.definition: An acid is a substance which produces hydrogen ions when dissolved in water
A base is a substance which produces hydroxide ions when dissolved in water
A salt is an ionic compound formed by the neutralization reaction between an acid and a base
pH is a scale used to measure the hydrogen ion concentration in a solution
Neutralization is a chemical reaction between an acid and a base to form salt and water

importantQuestions.question: Define acid and give two examples.
importantQuestions.answer: An acid is a substance which produces hydrogen ions when dissolved in water. Examples: Hydrochloric acid (HCl), Sulphuric acid (H2SO4)

importantQuestions.question: Explain the preparation of sodium chloride from hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide. Write chemical equation and describe the process.
importantQuestions.answer: Sodium chloride is prepared by neutralization reaction between hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide. Chemical equation: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H2O. Process: Take measured amount of HCl in a beaker. Add NaOH solution drop by drop until neutral. Evaporate water to get solid NaCl crystals. This is called neutralization because acid and base neutralize each other to form salt and water.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a strong acid?
The pH of pure water at 25°C is:
Which indicator turns red in acidic solution?

commonMistakes: Students confuse acids producing H+ ions with bases producing OH- ions
Writing wrong chemical formulas for common acids and bases
Forgetting to balance chemical equations in neutralization reactions
Mixing up pH values - remembering that below 7 is acidic, above 7 is basic
Not writing complete ionic equations when asked for salt preparation
examTips: This chapter usually has 1-2 MCQs and 1 short question in every BSEK paper
Always memorize exact definitions as they appear in STBB textbook
Practice writing chemical equations for neutralization reactions
Remember common examples of acids, bases and salts used in daily life
pH scale diagram often appears in exams - practice drawing it
note_data.keyDiagram: pH scale from 0 to 14 showing acidic range (0-7), neutral point (7), and basic range (7-14) with examples of common substances at different pH values like lemon juice, water, soap solution
pastPaperRefs: BSEK 2023 SSC-II Q4 (4 marks) — asked students to define acids and bases according to Arrhenius theory with examples
BSEK 2022 SSC-II Q6 (6 marks) — required explanation of neutralization reaction with chemical equation and salt formation
BSEK 2021 SSC-II Q3 (3 marks) — tested knowledge of indicators and their color changes in acidic and basic solutions
BSEK 2020 SSC-II Q5 (5 marks) — asked to classify salts as acidic, basic or neutral with reasoning and examples

workedExample.question: Define acid and base according to Arrhenius theory. Write the neutralization reaction between hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide. Name the salt formed. (6 marks)
steps: Write exact textbook definition of Arrhenius acid
Write exact textbook definition of Arrhenius base
Write balanced chemical equation for the neutralization reaction
Identify and name the salt formed
workedExample.answer: According to Arrhenius theory, an acid is a substance which ionizes in water to produce hydrogen ions (H⁺). A base is a substance which ionizes in water to produce hydroxide ions (OH⁻). The neutralization reaction is: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O. The salt formed is sodium chloride (NaCl).
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for acid definition, 1 mark for base definition, 2 marks for correct balanced equation, 1 mark for identifying NaCl, 1 mark for naming sodium chloride

examinerWarnings: Students often lose marks by writing general answers instead of textbook-exact definitions. Always use the STBB textbook phrasing for Arrhenius, Bronsted-Lowry definitions.
Many students forget to balance chemical equations properly in neutralization reactions. Always check that atoms and charges are balanced on both sides.
Avoid giving practical examples when asked for definitions only. BSEK wants precise theoretical definitions, not applications or everyday examples.
note_data.quickRecap: Acids produce H⁺ ions in water, bases produce OH⁻ ions (Arrhenius theory). Acids turn blue litmus red, bases turn red litmus blue. Neutralization: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Salts can be acidic, basic or neutral depending on their parent acid and base strength.
